name: Puloman
shortDescription: Puloman was a powerful Asura king, known for his conflict with Indra and ultimately defeated to protect his wife, Sachi.
description: Puloman, in Hindu mythology, is a prominent Asura king, a member of the powerful race of demons who frequently clashed with the Devas (gods). He is primarily known from accounts in the Mahabharata and various Puranas, where his interactions with Indra, the king of the gods, are detailed. Puloman's story is often linked to the protection of his wife, Sachi (also known as Indrani), and his ultimate demise at the hands of Indra. His significance lies in illustrating the ongoing cosmic battle between good and evil, and the lengths to which gods and demons would go to protect their interests and loved ones. The narrative surrounding Puloman highlights themes of power, protection, and the consequences of challenging divine authority. He serves as a cautionary figure within the larger framework of Hindu cosmology.
subtitle: Asura King, Sachi's Protector, Indra's Foe

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Puloman's origins are rooted in the lineage of Asuras, born from the sage Kashyapa and his wife Diti. He rose to become a formidable king, commanding a vast army and possessing significant magical abilities. The most prominent myth involving Puloman revolves around his desire for Sachi, Indra's wife. According to the accounts, Puloman attempted to abduct Sachi. This act incited Indra's wrath, leading to a fierce battle between the two powerful figures. The details vary across different texts, but the core narrative remains consistent: Puloman's ambition and aggression ultimately lead to his downfall at the hands of Indra, who defends his wife and maintains the cosmic order.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Puloman plays a significant role in illustrating the eternal conflict between the Devas and Asuras. He embodies the qualities often associated with Asuras: ambition, power, and a tendency to disrupt the established order. His story serves as a reminder of the importance of dharma (righteousness) and the consequences of adharma (unrighteousness). While Asuras are often portrayed as antagonists, their existence is crucial for maintaining the cosmic balance. Puloman's defeat reinforces the idea that even the most powerful beings cannot overcome the forces of righteousness when they are aligned with dharma. He also highlights the importance of protection and the duties associated with kingship and marriage.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Puloman symbolizes unchecked ambition and the dangers of lust and greed. His desire for Sachi represents a violation of dharma and a challenge to the established order. His defeat by Indra symbolizes the triumph of good over evil, and the restoration of cosmic balance. The story can be interpreted as an allegory for the internal struggles within individuals, where unchecked desires and negative emotions can lead to downfall. Puloman's character serves as a cautionary tale, urging individuals to control their impulses and adhere to the principles of dharma.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: While Puloman is not a widely worshipped deity, his story continues to be recounted in Hindu scriptures and oral traditions. He appears in various retellings of the Mahabharata and Puranas, often serving as a supporting character in narratives centered around Indra and other prominent deities. His legacy lies in his role as a representative of the Asura race and a symbol of the challenges faced by the gods in maintaining cosmic order. He also serves as a reminder of the importance of dharma and the consequences of succumbing to negative impulses. In modern interpretations, Puloman's story can be seen as a metaphor for the internal conflicts and moral dilemmas faced by individuals in their own lives.

sources.author: Vyasadeva
sources.description: The epic Mahabharata contains accounts of Puloman's interactions with Indra.

sources.author: Various Authors
sources.description: The Puranas provide detailed accounts of Asura kings and their conflicts with the Devas, including Puloman.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Represents the destructive power and rage associated with Asuras.

associations.name: Adharma
associations.significance: Puloman's actions embody the concept of adharma, or unrighteousness.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Typhon
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A monstrous, powerful figure who challenges the authority of the Olympian gods, similar to Puloman's challenge to Indra.

companions.name: Sachi (Indrani)
companions.description: Indra's wife, whose attempted abduction by Puloman leads to the conflict between them.

companions.name: Indra
companions.description: King of the Gods and Puloman's primary antagonist, who defeats him to protect Sachi.

cultural.modernLegacy: Puloman's story is primarily preserved through the retelling of Hindu epics and Puranas, where he serves as a cautionary figure. He is not typically the subject of modern worship practices, but his narrative continues to inform discussions about dharma, adharma, and the eternal struggle between good and evil.
